name: Formalize cross-language codegen manifest and lockfile support
description:
The ghawfr work also spawned a second substantive track: define a language-agnostic schema/codegen manifest format and deterministic lockfile format, then generate Python and Go bindings from the schemas themselves. The staged tree adds `schemas/codegen/`, a Python lockfile materializer in `lib/schema_codegen/lockfile.py`, generated Python models, Go codegen manifest/lockfile bindings under `ghawfr/devtools/`, `ghawfr-dev codegen lock`, and the new `nixcfg schema lock` CLI.

This work matters on its own because it formalizes the config contract that ghawfr and repo tooling can share across languages, and it also required strengthening the Python schema codegen pipeline (import normalization, class conflict handling, tests/golden fixtures).

Acceptance criteria:
- canonical manifest + lockfile schemas and lockfile spec docs are checked in under `schemas/codegen/`
- Python and Go tooling can materialize the same deterministic lockfile from the same manifest
- generated bindings and golden fixtures stay in sync under test
- nixcfg/ghawfr CLI entrypoints and docs make it clear when metadata is reproducible vs informational-only

name: Close remaining nixcfg CI runner gaps in ghawfr
description:
Continue the parity work for `.github/workflows/ci.yml`. Recent Pi sessions focused on runner-like file commands, cache save/restore behavior, remote/shared HOME path handling, host↔guest workspace translation, setup-python/setup-uv semantics, tool-cache behavior, and strict working-directory/path validation. The staged repo now also adds a `ghawfr-go` quality job to CI and dev-shell support for `go test ./...`.

The goal is not necessarily to make every repo test pass immediately, but to make CI job outcomes reflect actual repo problems instead of missing ghawfr action/runtime behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- current nixcfg CI jobs no longer fail because ghawfr is missing core runner semantics that the workflow relies on
- curated `uses:` support for the repo's common actions is documented and regression-tested
- path/home/tool-cache behavior is consistent across local and remote workers
- remaining CI failures, if any, are attributable to the underlying commands/tests rather than workflow-runner mismatches

name: Fix Darwin crate2nix Tauri env propagation for opencode-desktop
description: The crate2nix attempt for opencode-desktop is currently blocked on Darwin-specific Tauri metadata propagation under buildRustCrate. While unblocking argus system builds, we found that tauri and tauri-plugin build-script outputs depend on transient build/source paths, some generated DEP_TAURI_* identifiers are not valid shell exports, cargo metadata like cargo:dev must survive across derivations, and permission-files payloads contain nested source paths that also need rewriting. The immediate system-closure fix is to keep using the previous buildRustPackage-based package definition for opencode-desktop. Follow-up work should make the checked-in Cargo.nix workflow viable on Darwin without regressing the existing package behavior. Acceptance criteria: darwinConfigurations.argus.pkgs.opencode-desktop builds via crate2nix on Darwin, the resulting package matches current app behavior, and argus system builds remain green.

name: Incubate ghawfr for local nixcfg workflow execution
description:
Since the 2026-03-20 Pi sessions, the main in-flight initiative has been building `ghawfr/` as an incubating local GitHub Actions workflow runner and scheduler for this repo. The motivation is to reproduce and harden failures in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` and `.github/workflows/update.yml` locally instead of debugging only in hosted GitHub Actions runs.

The current staged tree includes:
- a new Go project under `ghawfr/` with workflow parsing, planning, controller/state, cache/artifact handling, action adapters, worker protocol, and provider routing
- host/local execution plus QEMU Linux and Virtualization.framework (`vz`) worker scaffolding
- repo integration such as `ghawfr-go` tests in CI/dev shell
- materialized `.ghawfr/` run outputs from actually exercising the workflows
- supporting cross-language schema/codegen work used by ghawfr tooling

Keep this initiative extractable into its own OSS repo, but make it useful enough in-tree to run nixcfg workflows from the ghawfr perspective. Done means the work is broken into clean, reviewable source changes, remaining parity gaps are explicit, and ghawfr can exercise the repo workflows without depending on GitHub-hosted runners for iteration.

name: Reach Periodic Flake Update workflow parity under ghawfr
description:
Use ghawfr to run `.github/workflows/update.yml` end-to-end from the local nixcfg checkout. The current sessions and staged tree show this is already far enough along to route Linux and Darwin jobs, materialize provider plans, and produce repo outputs such as `flake.lock`, `pinned-versions.json`, refreshed `sources.json` files, and crate2nix artifacts. There are also `.ghawfr/runs/update-*.json` records showing both successful and failing local update runs.

Focus on the remaining runner/runtime semantics needed so update workflow failures are real workflow/content issues rather than ghawfr bugs. Keep the repo-output files separate from `.ghawfr/artifacts/` execution leftovers.

Acceptance criteria:
- `ghawfr run .github/workflows/update.yml` (or the required selected jobs) succeeds from the ghawfr/runtime perspective on representative local runs
- update outputs copied back into the repo are the expected source files, not hidden runner state
- provider routing for `crate2nix-*`, `compute-hashes[*]`, `merge-hashes`, `resolve-versions`, and `update-lock` is documented well enough to debug future failures
- any still-unsupported workflow semantics are explicitly documented

name: Finish Zed crate2nix migration
description:
Complete the in-progress crate2nix packaging for zed-editor-nightly so local iteration and CI benefit from per-crate caching. There is already a draft implementation in packages/zed-editor-nightly/ with checked-in-style artifacts: default.nix, Cargo.nix, crate-hashes.json, normalize_cargo_nix.py, and README.md. The goal is to turn that draft into a working Darwin package that is faster to iterate on than the upstream flake/crane-based build.

Keep the repo pattern already used by Codex and Goose:
- checked-in Cargo.nix
- rootSrc = patchedSrc
- centralized crate overrides
- reuse upstream bundle metadata and assets where possible

Acceptance criteria:
- nix build for darwinConfigurations.argus.pkgs.zed-editor-nightly succeeds without linking
- the resulting zed binary smoke-tests cleanly, at least with --help
- any remaining blockers or unavoidable follow-up work are documented explicitly
result: Finished the Darwin crate2nix packaging for zed-editor-nightly in the working tree. Replaced the macOS app icon generation step that depended on sandbox-unavailable sips/iconutil with Nix-provided imagemagick plus libicns/png2icns in packages/zed-editor-nightly/default.nix. Verification: nix build .#darwinConfigurations.argus.pkgs.zed-editor-nightly --no-link succeeded and produced /nix/store/vq8pmf2156d2hnzxajs09a4nkkp0rbm9-zed-editor-nightly-unstable-8a7daea; the bundled app binary at Applications/Zed Nightly.app/Contents/MacOS/zed --help succeeded; the packaged CLI at bin/zed --help succeeded; file -L on the generated icns confirmed a Mac OS X icon file. Note: libicns still prints non-fatal warnings during icon generation, but the build completes and the icon artifact is valid.

name: Improve heavy Rust build caching with crate2nix
description: Track crate2nix adoption for the repo's heaviest Rust-heavy packages. The immediate priority is finishing the Zed crate2nix migration to improve rebuild times. Follow-up Rust candidates should be recorded here so they are not lost while Zed is the focus. Success for this initiative means Zed lands with a working high-cacheability crate2nix build, and any remaining worthwhile candidates are captured with enough context to revisit later.

name: Revisit other Rust-y crate2nix candidates after Zed
description:
Record the non-Zed Rust-ish packages that might be worth moving to crate2nix later, so we can stay focused on Zed now without losing the analysis.

Current recommendation set:
- opencode-desktop is the strongest follow-up candidate because it is a Rust/Tauri build currently using buildRustPackage plus Tauri git-dep hashing and sidecar wiring; per-crate caching would likely help
- scratch is a lower-priority maybe: also Tauri/Rust, but probably smaller so the maintenance tradeoff is less clear
- sentry-cli is probably not worth crate2nix because the current fetchCargoVendor approach is likely sufficient
- opencode itself is primarily Bun/JS-oriented, so the desktop backend is the real Rust target rather than the main package
- codex-desktop is a binary app, not a source Rust target
- gitbutler was previously investigated, but the repo history leaned toward crane or other approaches and later dropped the package from Nix, so it should not be treated as an active crate2nix target

Revisit this list only after Zed is in good shape.

name: Migrate opencode-desktop to crate2nix
description: Migrate opencode-desktop from buildRustPackage to a checked-in crate2nix workflow, following the repo patterns used by Codex, Goose, and Zed where they make sense. The package is a Rust/Tauri app and is the strongest remaining non-Zed crate2nix candidate in the repo. Goals: generate and check in Cargo.nix and any required support files, keep the package building on Darwin, preserve the existing desktop app packaging behavior, and improve rebuild ergonomics through per-crate caching. Acceptance criteria: nix build for darwinConfigurations.argus.pkgs.opencode-desktop succeeds, the packaged binary or app smoke-tests cleanly if practical, and any unavoidable follow-up work is documented.

name: Upstream Darwin optimiser skip for flat .app bundles
description: Local nixcfg now carries a patch that broadens Darwin store optimiser bundle skipping from only \.app/Contents/** to any nested \.app/** path, fixing EPERM when optimise/gc encounters flat app bundles such as expo-mcp's AutomationApp.app/_CodeSignature/CodeResources. Follow up by upstreaming the change to Nix (issue context: NixOS/nix#2229 and current optimise-store.cc Darwin workaround), and add or identify a regression test covering flat app bundle layouts on macOS.

name: Land ghawfr core runtime/backends as clean source-only changes
description:
Turn the current `ghawfr/` spike into a cleanly landable source change. The staged tree currently mixes real source code with runtime state under `.ghawfr/` (artifacts, run state, worker plans, cloud-init ISO, generated SSH keys/tool-cache entries). We need to preserve the actual ghawfr source packages (`workflow`, `planner`, `controller`, `backend`, `actionadapter`, `cache`, `state`, `workerproto`, CLIs, README/tests) while preventing ephemeral runtime outputs and secrets from being committed accidentally.

Also decide whether incidental repo tweaks currently staged alongside ghawfr (for example the Chrome DevTools MCP/profile changes and the `coreutils-full` switch) belong in separate commits/tasks instead of being buried in the ghawfr landing.

Acceptance criteria:
- `ghawfr/` source, docs, and tests are reviewable without `.ghawfr/` runtime debris
- accidental secrets/runtime files are removed or ignored appropriately
- repo quality checks include the intended Go test coverage for ghawfr
- any intentionally retained generated assets are documented as source-of-truth artifacts rather than execution leftovers
